textPlain: In some ways, a single blade is the most efficient. Not very good mechanically though!

There are companies working on two blade design, which has other benefits. You can try to get rid of balance problem with mechanical design and software:https://youtu.be/GbeFYLI8zYg?si=32wECCs05c4rRfs6
Is it related to automobile wheels always seeming to have odd-numbered spokes?

TL,DR: three blades (1) are a good balance of torque against drag at different speeds, (2) help reduce unbalanced forces compared to two-blade turbines, and (3) provide visual balance, smoother motion, and quieter operation to improve community acceptance.

In Australia, remember that each windmill you see costs the tax payer in the order of 500k per year in subsidies.Edit : added reference and the actual figure of close to 500Khttps://www.aph.gov.au/parliamentary_business/committees/sen...

We had the Growian two blade designs and its successor the Monopteros single blade design in the 80s. They were pretty famous back then and looked spectacular because of their sheer size and the unexpected asymmetry. Unfortunately they are almost forgotten now.
Reminds me of Dyson, who makes zero-blade fans.https://www.dyson.com/air-treatment/fans-heaters

That has to do with the physics of harmonic vibration.It's not that you can't make even numbers of spokes balanced, it's just marginally easier to do odd numbers.Back in ye-olden days when things sucked there were more manufacturing benefits to it too but these days (this century) that doesn't really matter because even the sloppiest casting factory in china has their stuff figured out.
There are plenty of six spoke wheels?
